questions.question: List the four fundamental components of an IoT device and state the primary function of each.
keyPoints: Sensor/Actuator: Interfaces with the physical world - sensors measure the environment (input), actuators perform actions (output).
Microcontroller/Microprocessor: The computational brain that processes data and controls all other components.
Connectivity Module: Enables communication with networks and other devices via WiFi, Bluetooth, LoRa, cellular, etc.
Power Supply: Provides the necessary energy via batteries, mains power, solar, or energy harvesting.

questions.question: Compare and contrast Microcontroller Units (MCUs) and Microprocessor Units (MPUs). Discuss their architecture, typical use cases in IoT, and provide one example of each.
keyPoints: Architecture: MCU is a SoC with integrated CPU, memory (RAM/Flash), and I/O peripherals on a single chip. MPU is a standalone CPU requiring external chips for memory and peripherals.
Power Consumption: MCUs are generally more power-efficient than MPUs due to integrated design and simpler architecture.
Processing Power: MPUs are typically more powerful and can run full operating systems like Linux.
Use Case: MCUs are for dedicated, low-power tasks (e.g., sensor node reading temperature). MPUs are for complex processing and applications (e.g., smart camera with image recognition).
Examples: MCU - ESP32, Arduino Uno (simple real-time control). MPU - Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone (gateway, edge computing).

questions.question: Explain the four-layer IoT architecture model with the role of each layer.
keyPoints: Perception Layer: Physical devices with sensors and actuators that interact with the environment to collect data.
Network Layer: Handles data transport using protocols like WiFi, LoRaWAN, cellular, Zigbee to move data between devices and cloud.
Processing Layer: Cloud or edge computing platforms that store, process, and analyze IoT data using databases and analytics engines.
Application Layer: User-facing applications, dashboards, mobile apps that present insights and enable control of IoT systems.

questions.question: Discuss the various connectivity technologies available for IoT devices. Compare short-range and long-range protocols and explain how to select the appropriate technology for a given IoT application.
keyPoints: Short-range protocols: WiFi (high bandwidth, 100m range, high power), BLE (low power, 10m, low data rate), Zigbee (mesh networking, 100m, low power), NFC (very short range, contactless payments).
Long-range protocols (LPWAN): LoRaWAN (10+ km, ultra-low power, small payloads), NB-IoT (wide coverage via cellular infrastructure, moderate power), Sigfox (similar to LoRa, simpler protocol).
Cellular: 4G/LTE and 5G for high-bandwidth applications like video streaming, autonomous vehicles.

Selection criteria: Range needed (indoor vs rural), data volume (bytes vs megabytes), power budget (battery vs mains), latency requirements (real-time vs daily), cost per device, existing infrastructure availability.
Example: Smart home uses WiFi/BLE (short range, infrastructure available). Farm sensors use LoRaWAN (long range, low power, rural). Connected car uses 5G (high bandwidth, low latency).
